figures.gloss: The absolute, indivisible Creator and Sustainer of the heavens and the earth. He is unbegotten, unbegetting, and beyond all human comprehension, governing the cosmos entirely through His merciful and omnipotent will.
figures.facet: Tawhid (absolute oneness) is so uncompromising that associating partners with Him (shirk) is the ultimate theological corruption. He has no sons, no avatars, and no dualistic rivals; He is simultaneously utterly transcendent and closer to a person than their jugular vein.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an; Hadith Qudsi; classical Kalām (Islamic theology).

figures.gloss: The literal, uncreated speech of Allah, revealed sequentially to Muhammad through the archangel Jibril. It is not merely a record of divine inspiration but the infallible, phonetic presence of God's mind in human history.
figures.facet: Unlike Christianity where the Word became flesh, here the Word became text. The Arabic recitation itself is a sacramental act, placing the vibrational weight of divine reality directly into the human throat.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an; orthodox Ash'ari and Maturidi theology.

figures.gloss: The final messenger sent to humanity, concluding the long chain of Abrahamic prophethood. He bore the ultimate revelation, established the final covenant, and ascended through the heavens to the divine presence during the Isra and Mi'raj.
figures.facet: He is emphatically defended as purely human—he performs no salvific miracles by his own power and shares no divine essence. Yet his lived example (sunnah) serves as the perfect, eternal template for behavior, merging mundane daily hygiene with cosmic destiny.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an; Sahih Hadith collections; classical Sira (prophetic biographies).

figures.gloss: The cubic black stone sanctuary in Mecca, built by Abraham and Ishmael, serving as the qibla (direction of prayer) for all Muslims. It is the earthly reflection of the celestial house circumambulated by angels.
figures.facet: It contains no idol and no icon; believers prostrate toward an empty center. It acts as the gravitational core of a global ritual matrix, physically aligning millions of bodies synchronously every day.

figures.gloss: The entity who refused God's command to prostrate before the newly created Adam out of pride in his own fiery origin. Cast out, he acts as the tempter, permitted by God to test humanity until the Day of Judgment.
figures.facet: Iblis is not a fallen angel (who lack free will) but a jinn. His rebellion is not a genuine cosmic threat to God's throne but a permitted, strictly bounded trial. His fatal flaw was not desiring godhood, but racist pride ('I am better than him: You created me from fire and him from clay').

figures.gloss: The esoteric and exoteric leadership of the Muslim community, vested exclusively in the purified lineage of the Prophet through Fatima and Ali. The Imams are the infallible guides and living interpreters of the Qur'an's inner meaning.
figures.facet: While legislative revelation ended with Muhammad, divine guidance could not. The Imam serves as the cosmic pole (Qutb) without which the earth would shatter, ensuring a continuous, physical human bloodline carries the indispensable light of esoteric truth.
figures.provenance: Nahj al-Balagha; Usul al-Kafi; foundational Shia theology.

figures.gloss: The mystical Sufi state of 'annihilation' in God, where the seeker's individual ego and will are entirely subsumed into the divine presence, achieved through intensive dhikr (remembrance) and ascetic discipline.
figures.facet: It dances on the razor's edge of orthodoxy. While adhering to strict monotheism, it insists the ultimate realization of Tawhid is the obliteration of the 'I'—not becoming God, but realizing the individual self was always an illusion masking the One.
figures.provenance: Writings of Rumi, Ibn Arabi, Al-Ghazali; early Sufi manuals.

figures.gloss: The obligatory pilgrimage to Mecca, ritually reenacting the trials of Abraham, Hagar, and Ishmael. Pilgrims gather on the plain of Arafat in a vast rehearsal of the Day of Judgment.
figures.facet: It enforces a radical, physical egalitarianism. Kings and paupers wear the exact same unstitched white cloth, anchoring an abstract, transcendent monotheism to extreme physical exertion, heat, and visceral geographic specificity.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an; Sunnah; consensus of Islamic jurisprudence.

figures.gloss: The archangel who transmitted the Qur'an to Muhammad piecemeal across 23 years of intermittent revelation, appearing in his true, horizon-filling form on at least two occasions (Surah al-Najm), and identified by exegetes with the 'Trustworthy Spirit' (al-Ruh al-Amin) of Surah al-Shu'ara. He also guided Muhammad through the seven heavens during the Mi'raj.
figures.facet: Revelation given a named, visible intermediary rather than left as unmediated inward inspiration: Islam insists a created angel — not God himself — carries the message, which keeps the channel of transmission firmly outside the object of worship even as the message itself is held to be God's literal, uncreated speech.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an (2:97, 53:5-18, 26:193); Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim hadith on the Mi'raj; classical tafsir (al-Tabari, Ibn Kathir).

figures.gloss: A prophet and messenger of God, born miraculously of the virgin Maryam by divine command, given the Gospel (Injil), and empowered to heal the sick and raise the dead by God's leave alone. Mainstream tafsir on Q4:157 holds that he was not crucified but raised bodily to God, with someone made to resemble him crucified in his place; he is expected to return before the Last Day to defeat al-Dajjal.
figures.facet: The strongest possible prophetic Christology short of divinity: Islam grants Isa a virgin birth, nature miracles, and a future Second Coming, yet removes exactly the two claims — death by crucifixion and divine sonship — that Christianity holds indispensable, leaving an otherwise nearly identical biography.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an (Surah Maryam 19; Al 'Imran 3:45-55; al-Nisa 4:157-159); classical tafsir on the crucifixion verse (al-Tabari, Ibn Kathir); Sahih Muslim hadith on his eschatological return.

figures.gloss: The unnamed 'righteous servant' of Surah al-Kahf (18:60-82), identified in hadith and tafsir as al-Khidr. Musa seeks him out for hidden knowledge and witnesses three apparently unjust acts — scuttling a boat, killing a youth, repairing a wall without payment — each later revealed as concealed mercy. In Sufi tradition he is the immortal initiator who appears directly to saints outside the formal chain of transmission (silsila).
figures.facet: Knowledge that overrides visible law: Khidr's gnosis (ma'rifa) contradicts Musa's shari'a-bound expectations at every turn, making him Islam's own internal acknowledgment that revealed law and hidden wisdom can look, from the outside, like injustice.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an, Surah al-Kahf 18:60-82; Sahih al-Bukhari's hadith identifying the 'servant' as al-Khidr; classical Sufi literature (Ibn Arabi, al-Qushayri) on Khidr as an extra-silsila initiator.

figures.gloss: A category of beings named throughout the Qur'an (notably Surah 72, al-Jinn), created from 'smokeless fire,' possessing free will and moral accountability like humans, and living largely unseen alongside humanity. Some are believers, some rebellious; the category includes Iblis, explicitly identified in the Qur'an (18:50) as one of the jinn rather than a fallen angel.
figures.facet: A third rational species built into the cosmos as a structural necessity, not an afterthought: Iblis's entire theological function — a being with genuine free will capable of genuine refusal — depends on this class of creature existing prior to and independent of his own story.
figures.provenance: The Qur'an (Surah al-Jinn 72; al-Hijr 15:27; al-Rahman 55:15; al-Kahf 18:50); classical kalam on the ontology of jinn (al-Ash'ari, al-Baydawi).

figures.gloss: The white riding-beast described in hadith as 'smaller than a mule, larger than a donkey,' each stride reaching to the horizon of its sight — the mount that carried Muhammad from the Kaaba to Jerusalem's Temple Mount (al-Isra) in a single night, from where he ascended through the heavens (al-Mi'raj).
figures.facet: Distance collapsed rather than dispensed with: Buraq does not remove the journey's physicality — the Prophet still rides, still travels — it compresses a caravan-month between Mecca and Jerusalem into one night, making the miracle a matter of speed rather than of leaving the body behind.
figures.provenance: Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim hadith on al-Isra wal-Mi'raj; classical Sira literature (Ibn Ishaq via Ibn Hisham).

figures.gloss: The 'rightly guided one' awaited to appear before the Day of Judgment and fill the earth with justice as it had been filled with tyranny — attested across Sunni hadith collections though absent from the Qur'an itself. In Twelver Shi'ism he is identified specifically with Muhammad al-Mahdi, the Twelfth Imam, held to have entered occultation (ghayba) in 874 CE and to remain alive, hidden, until his return.
figures.facet: Sunni and Shia eschatology converge on the office but diverge on the person: for Sunnis the Mahdi is a future figure yet to be born, for Twelvers he is already alive and has been for over a millennium — one title naming either an open expectation or a completed, waiting identity.
figures.provenance: Sunan Abu Dawud and Sunan Ibn Majah (hadith on the Mahdi); Twelver Shi'i doctrine of the occultation in al-Kulayni's Usul al-Kafi and Shaykh al-Saduq's Kamal al-Din.

figures.gloss: The one-eyed false messiah of the Last Days, extensively described in the sahih hadith: he will emerge claiming divinity, perform deceptive wonders (a counterfeit paradise and hell, abundant rain and harvest for followers), and bear the word 'kafir' (disbeliever) legibly on his forehead for the discerning believer to read. He is destroyed by Isa ibn Maryam at the gate of Lud.
figures.facet: An adversary who wins by counterfeit rather than by force: unlike a chaos-monster met in single combat, al-Dajjal's danger is epistemic — his test is discernment between the true and the convincingly false, not physical resistance.
figures.provenance: Sahih Muslim (the extended hadith of Tamim al-Dari); Sahih al-Bukhari; Sunan Abu Dawud; classical eschatological compendia (Ibn Kathir).

figures.gloss: The canonical inventory of God's 'most beautiful names' (al-Rahman, al-Rahim, al-'Adl, al-Latif...) drawn from Qur'anic epithets and systematized in hadith and classical theology into a fixed list of ninety-nine; recited on the misbaha (prayer beads) in dhikr, and the subject of a hadith promising Paradise to whoever 'enumerates' them.
figures.facet: Tawhid made practicable: the doctrine of God's absolute, undifferentiated oneness would otherwise leave devotion nothing concrete to hold; the Names translate that abstraction into ninety-nine specific handles a worshipper can count and call on one at a time.
figures.provenance: Sahih al-Bukhari and Sunan al-Tirmidhi (the enumeration hadith); al-Ghazali, al-Maqsad al-Asna fi Sharh Asma' Allah al-Husna.

figures.gloss: The five prescribed daily prayers — dawn, midday, afternoon, sunset, and night — each preceded by ritual ablution (wudu) and consisting of a fixed cycle of standing recitation, bowing, and prostration performed facing the Kaaba. Islamic tradition places its institution during Muhammad's Mi'raj, when an original fifty daily prayers were negotiated down to five (while retaining the reward of fifty) through the Prophet's repeated intercession at Musa's urging.
figures.facet: The pillar the ascension narrative exists to deliver: the Mi'raj is not only a mystical vision but the origin story of a precise, recurring liturgical obligation — five fixed times a day, facing one point on earth — that synchronizes the entire global umma's bodies in daily unison.
figures.provenance: Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim (the Mi'raj hadith negotiating fifty prayers to five); the Qur'an (17:78, 11:114) on prescribed prayer times; classical fiqh manuals on salat's form.

edges.note: The Qur'an is the uncreated speech of Allah, serving as His direct, active presence in the material world.
Muhammad is the unlettered human vessel perfectly chosen to receive and transmit the divine text.
Iblis represents the ultimate rejection of Allah's sovereign command, choosing personal pride over divine submission.
The Hajj is the active, kinetic ritual whose entire geographic logic is anchored by the physical structure of the Kaaba.
GOLD SEAM: The explicit, documented theological identification of the God of the Qur'an with the God of Abraham and Moses.
GOLD SEAM: Muhammad revolutionized the pre-Islamic High God of the Quraysh by stripping away all associated partner deities to assert strict monotheism.
GOLD SEAM: Structurally, the Qur'an occupies the exact theological space in Islam that Christ does in Christianity: the eternal, uncreated Word of God entering the physical world.
GOLD SEAM: Both prophetic figures explicitly claimed the title of the 'Seal of the Prophets', bringing a final revelation to correct the corrupted texts of previous traditions.
GOLD SEAM: The cosmic adversary who seeks to ruin humanity, though Islam strictly denies him the co-eternal, dualistic power held by the Zoroastrian destructive spirit.
GOLD SEAM: The literal integration of the pre-Islamic Meccan sacred center and its meteorite into the heart of the new monotheistic framework.
GOLD SEAM: Both systems locate ultimate liberation in the extinguishing of the illusion of the independent ego, though Sufism subsumes it into the Divine presence rather than a void.
GOLD SEAM: Both are fiery, primordial beings who interrupt the creator's intended cosmic order through a premature act of rebellious pride.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the absolute, indivisible source of reality that entirely transcends human categorization and physical incarnation.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, non-dual reality underpinning all existence, a parallel drawn explicitly in later Persian and Indian Sufi metaphysics (Wahdat al-Wujud).
GOLD SEAM: The architectural and spiritual center of the world system, operating as the cosmic navel around which believers perform ritual circumambulation.
GOLD SEAM: The Prophet's Night Journey (Mi'raj) through the heavens parallels the ecstatic shamanic ascent, though here used to finalize the universal law of prayer.
GOLD SEAM: The Shia concept of a continuous, physically transmitted lineage of divine authority echoes foundational archetypes of an unsevered bloodline carrying world-sustaining power.
GOLD SEAM: The pilgrimage operates as a temporal return to the very beginning, ritually reenacting the primal actions of Abraham and Hagar to reset the believer's spiritual clock.
GOLD SEAM: The Jesus Prayer and Sufi dhikr are parallel disciplines of unceasing divine-name repetition dissolving the ego toward union — a long-noted structural kinship, not a documented borrowing.

GOLD SEAM: under Islam the iskoki survive by reclassification as aljanu — the Qur'anic jinn-class to which Iblis belongs — orthodoxy's own demonology becoming the cover under which the old pantheon persists.

GOLD SEAM: bori's map of its own Islamization — the order of Allah absorbed as the 'white' Muslim moiety of the pantheon while the pagan 'black' spirits keep the bush; a tension seam, not an equation.

GOLD SEAM: like bori's iskoki, the zayran live inside Islam as a species of jinn — Boddy's Sudanese hosts class them as the 'red wind' — Qur'anic demonology licensing a women's cult that Islamic reformers condemn.

GOLD SEAM: Ghulam Ahmad claimed only a derivative, non-law-bearing prophethood under Muhammad's seal — the subordination itself is the doctrine, and the reading of that seal is the exact wound Pakistan's anti-Ahmadi laws legislate.
GOLD SEAM: Both answer the same wound — how divine guidance continues after the founder — one through an infallible bloodline, the other through an elected caliphate held to be divinely guided.
GOLD SEAM: The Báb's initial 1844 claim was to be the gate of the Hidden Twelfth Imam — Twelver Shi'i expectation, sharpened by the Shaykhi school, is the soil the movement grew from.
GOLD SEAM: The Bayán is modeled on the Qur'an's station while explicitly claiming to abrogate its dispensation — successor scripture, not commentary.
GOLD SEAM: Every silsila terminates in the Prophet (most via 'Ali) — Sufism is Islam's interior discipline claiming direct descent, not a separate religion beside it.
GOLD SEAM: Fana, annihilation in the Beloved, is the terminus of dhikr practice — the parent shard already carries Sufism's crown concept; one seam, same body.
GOLD SEAM: The Sevener imamate descends directly from the Shi'i doctrine of divinely designated leadership, branching at Ja'far al-Sadiq's succession in 765 — proven doctrinal descent within Islam.
As Jibril, this same messenger is credited in Islamic doctrine with delivering the Qur'an to Muhammad across twenty-three years of revelation beginning at Hira.
The Qur'an states outright that Iblis was 'of the jinn' (Q 18:50), not a fallen angel — Islam's own devil is drawn from this pre-Islamic spirit-class rather than invented anew.
The Qur'an explicitly denies Muhammad's revelation is kahin-speech (Q 69:42) — prophethood is defined here by negation of the older oracular office, not by continuity with it.
The hanif is the Qur'an's own retrojected monotheist category; Islam claims the hanifs' worship of 'the God of Abraham' as its true, uncorrupted precursor to submission to Allah.

Muzdalifah, where hajj pilgrims still halt overnight between Arafat and Mina, was Quzah's pre-Islamic fire-station; the site and its overnight rite carried into the Islamic pilgrimage though the storm-god's name did not.
The sa'i between Safa and Marwa, still run today in hajj and umrah, was the pre-Islamic idols' own sacrificial running-course.
A commentary that reworks and imitates Qur'anic verse from within, staged as a successor-text rather than an ordinary tafsir.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a single fixed geographic point toward which the whole faith's obligatory prayer is oriented, structurally the same office as a Qiblih though historically unconnected.
the Qur'an explicitly presents itself as confirming and correcting the Torah and the Gospel (Injil) that preceded it.
Jibril is the named transmission channel of the uncreated speech — the Qur'an entry already presupposes him without naming him.
Jibril delivers the revelation to Muhammad across 23 years and guides him through the Mi'raj — messenger and recipient, defined by the same events.
Isa's eschatological return exists specifically to destroy al-Dajjal — the returning prophet and the false messiah are direct textual opposites within the same end-times narrative.
In the hadith accounts, Isa descends and prays behind al-Mahdi rather than leading himself — the two end-times figures are cooperating parts of a single sequence, not rivals.
In Sufi hagiography Khidr initiates saints directly, unbound by any human silsila — the one widely attested exception to the chain of transmission the tariqa otherwise requires.
Iblis is explicitly a jinn (Q18:50), not a fallen angel — his entire rebellion depends on this category of free-willed beings existing.
Buraq is the named mount of the Prophet's Night Journey — creature and rider inseparable in the one narrative both belong to.
Both are awaited restorer-figures who set the world right at history's end — a structural analogy long noted in comparative eschatology, no direct descent claimed here.
Shared Abrahamic messianic office, differently instantiated — an anointed restorer of justice awaited at the end of the present order.

Ahmadis explicitly identify Mirza Ghulam Ahmad as the fulfillment of the hadith-predicted Mahdi (and Promised Messiah) — a documented doctrinal claim, not mere resemblance, and precisely the claim that separates Ahmadiyya from mainstream Islam.
The deceiver and the rightly guided one are the Islamic end-times drama's two poles, named together throughout the eschatological hadith.
The Names are the devotional inventory of the same absolute God whose oneness the Allah entry describes in the abstract.
The Names are dhikr's primary content — reciting and counting them is the standard form the remembrance practice takes.
Salat's fixed orientation toward the Kaaba is what turns the structure into the 'ritual matrix' aligning a global community's bodies in unison.
The rite's institution is narrated as an event in Muhammad's own biography — negotiated down from fifty to five prayers during the Mi'raj.
Both are obligatory pillars enforcing the same radical, physical enactment of tawhid — one performed five times daily by the individual body, the other once in a lifetime by the collective body.
Both describe a rare, ineffable dissolution of the boundary between self and ultimate reality, reached through disciplined contemplative practice.

Both scriptures are held by their own orthodoxy to be uncreated/eternal rather than authored — the Vedic apauruṣeyatva doctrine and the classical Ash'ari position on the Qur'an's uncreatedness are independent formulations of the same claim about sacred speech.

The ghulat floor beneath the ceiling: Twelver Imamah holds Ali a human, divinely-guided successor, while the Ahl-e Haqq avatar-cycle (already linked to Alawite and Alevi deification) makes him one incarnate body of the same Divine Essence.

Same twelve historical Imams from Ali to al-Mahdi carried forward from Twelver Shi'ism's Imamah doctrine, but reframed: orthodox Shi'ism holds them as divinely-guided historical office-holders, Alevism as luminous presences invoked structurally through the cem's twelve services.

A documented name-loan, not shared theology: 'Lahatala' is an Islamic-tinged form of Allah Ta'ala laid over the indigenous Upperworld lord, who structurally remains only the male half of Ngaju religion's bisexual godhead-dyad.

The Qur'an reworks the shared narrative of the angel commanded to bow before Adam who refused into a proud jinn merely permitted to tempt, not a fallen angel at cosmic war — same adversary-name, documented scriptural divergence.

Same narrative referent — the angel commanded to bow to Adam who refused — with opposite valuation: Islam reads the refusal as damnation (Iblis), Yazidism as the supreme proof of monotheist loyalty; the equation is external polemic Yazidism itself rejects, denying any Satan exists in its theology.

A documented name-loan only: Kapampangan/Tagalog Sitan borrows the name of Islamic Shaytān/Iblis via Bruneian Islam, but the transactional underworld keeper it names carries none of the jinn-tempter theology of the source.

Orthodox Islam's final human messenger is absorbed in Alawite doctrine into the Ali-Muhammad-Salman triad as the Ism, a subordinate emanation veiling Ali's Essence — the same prophet re-ranked within a suprahuman hierarchy orthodoxy does not recognize.

Orthodox Islam's final human messenger becomes, in the Alevi Hak-Muham-med-Ali triad, the exoteric face of a single divine unity with Ali — the same prophet held inside a triadic cosmology orthodoxy rejects.

Islam's Seal of the Prophets, closing revelation, becomes in Baha'i teaching one mirror among an endless series of Manifestations (Abraham, Krishna, Buddha, Christ, Muhammad, Baha'u'llah) — the same figure's finality claim directly denied.

dossier.essence: The monotheistic religion founded on the prophethood of Muhammad (c. 570–632 CE) in Arabia, whose roughly two billion adherents (Muslims) hold that God (Allah) revealed the Qur'an to him through the angel Jibril, completing the line of Abrahamic prophecy. Its central claim is tawhid — the absolute oneness of God — and submission (islam) to God's will. It is divided principally into Sunni and Shia branches, with Sufism as its mystical dimension.
dossier.cosmos: God is the sole, indivisible creator, transcendent yet 'closer than the jugular vein,' who made the world and will judge it. History runs from creation to a final Day of Judgment when the dead are resurrected bodily and assigned to Paradise (janna) or Hell (jahannam) according to faith and deeds. Alongside humans, God created angels (lacking free will) and jinn (possessing it, made from fire); Iblis, a jinn who refused to bow to Adam out of prideful self-regard, is the permitted tempter until the end of time.
dossier.practice: Sunni Islam rests on the Five Pillars — the confession of faith (shahada), five daily prayers (salat) toward the Kaaba in Mecca, almsgiving (zakat), the Ramadan fast, and the Hajj pilgrimage — within a comprehensive law (sharia) derived by jurists from Qur'an and prophetic example. Community life is shaped by the mosque, the scholars (ulama), and emulation of Muhammad's sunnah in even mundane conduct; Shia Islam adds the authority of the Imams (Ahl al-Bayt), and Sufi orders pursue annihilation of the ego in God (fana) through remembrance (dhikr). There is no priesthood or sacrament in the Christian sense.
dossier.canon: The Qur'an, held to be the uncreated speech of God, is primary; it is supplemented by the Hadith (reports of Muhammad's words and deeds, in canonical collections such as Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim) and the Sira (prophetic biography), which together ground Islamic law and theology.
dossier.reflects: Uncompromising monotheism — divine oneness pressed so far that associating any partner with God (shirk) is the cardinal sin, and revelation enters the world as recited text rather than incarnate flesh.
sources: Marshall G. S. Hodgson, 'The Venture of Islam' (1974)
W. Montgomery Watt, 'Muhammad: Prophet and Statesman' (1961)
